title: The Story of Yusuf: The Most Beautiful Story

captions.youtube:
The Story of Yusuf is the only chapter in the Qur'an that tells a single story from beginning to end, and Allah Himself calls it the most beautiful of stories (12:3). This is a faithful, fully sourced retelling of Surah Yusuf, with the lesser-known authentic details most retellings leave out: his lineage of four prophets in a row (Sahih al-Bukhari), that he was given half of all beauty (Sahih Muslim), his call to tawhid inside the prison (12:39), his refusal to leave prison until his name was cleared and the Prophet's words about that patience (Sahih al-Bukhari), Ya'qub's grief (12:84-86), the scent of Yusuf (12:94), and the way the Prophet echoed 'no blame upon you today' at the Conquest of Makkah. Seven verses are shown on screen with translation. Nothing is embellished; every claim is on-screen sourced to the Qur'an or to Sahih al-Bukhari and Sahih Muslim.

Sources: Surah Yusuf (Qur'an 12), text from Quran.com; Sahih al-Bukhari (3390 lineage; 3372 his patience in prison); Sahih Muslim (162, given half of beauty); the Conquest of Makkah from the books of seerah. Arabic is shown with translation, never recited or synthesized.

captions.shorts: Betrayed, enslaved, imprisoned, and every step was carrying him exactly where Allah meant him to be. The Story of Yusuf.

_note: Long-form 16:9 (~12 min). Pillar: Prophet stories (Tuesday long-form). Faithful, deeply sourced retelling of Surah Yusuf. Every claim is shown on screen with its source (foot): Qur'an by verse, hadith by collection (Sahih al-Bukhari 3390 lineage; Sahih al-Bukhari 3372 his patience in prison; Sahih Muslim 162 half of beauty), and the Conquest of Makkah from seerah. segments.text: Of all the stories in the Qur'an, only one is told from beginning to end, in a single chapter. And Allah Himself calls it the most beautiful of them all. This is the story of Yusuf, alayhi salam.
segments.title: THE STORY OF YUSUF

segments.text: Eleven stars, and the sun, and the moon, all bowing down before a young boy as he sleeps. Frightened and amazed, Yusuf, alayhi salam, runs to his father, the Prophet Yaqub, alayhi salam.
A father's hand closes gently over his small son's. He knows what a dream like this means. This boy has been chosen. Keep it between us, he warns him. Do not breathe a word of it to your brothers.

Out in the fields, his brothers seethe. Grown men, strong and many, and still their father's heart leans toward this one boy. They cannot forgive him for it. And quietly, that envy hardens into a plan.
Far from home, out in the open, they throw him down into the darkness of a deep well. Then they stand at the rim, and listen to a child cry out below them, and they turn and walk away.

A wolf, they tell their father that night, had taken Yusuf while they raced ahead. They hold up his shirt, stained with blood. But Yaqub knows his own sons are lying. And he turns to beautiful patience.

A passing caravan, crossing the dunes, stops at that well to draw water. And in the bucket, they find a boy. They carry him down to Egypt, and sell him there, for a few cheap coins, as if he were worth nothing.
A prophet's son, of the line of Ibrahim, carried hundreds of miles from his father, and sold in the markets of a foreign land.

Inside a grand house in Egypt, behind towering columns, Yusuf is bought by one of the most powerful men in the land, who tells his wife, take good care of him. And there, the boy grows into a young man.
And he grows into no ordinary man. On the night the Prophet, peace be upon him, was raised up through the heavens, he met Yusuf, and told us he had been given half of all the beauty in creation.
Down the long corridors of that house, the minister's wife closes the doors, one after another, until there is nowhere left for him to go. For years she had wanted him. And now she turns to him.
He turns toward the light, and refuses her. I seek refuge in Allah, he says. Your husband has trusted me, and treated me well. How could I ever wrong him? How could I disobey my Lord?
His shirt tears in her hands as he runs for the door. And just as it flies open, her husband is standing on the other side.

At a banquet, she places a knife and a piece of fruit in every woman's hand, and calls Yusuf into the room. The moment they see him, they are so overcome that they cut their own hands, and never feel the blade.

Prison, or obey her. That is her threat. And Yusuf chooses the prison. My Lord, he prays, it is dearer to me than the sin they call me to. And though he had done no wrong, the iron doors close behind him.

Two young men are thrown into the cell beside him, each carrying a dream he cannot understand. They come to Yusuf for answers. But before he reads a single dream, he turns them to something far greater.
O my two companions of prison. Are many scattered lords better, or Allah, the One, the Supreme?
One of you, he tells them, will press the grapes and pour wine for the king once more. The other will not survive. And to the one who will live, Yusuf says softly, remember me to your master.
But the freed man forgets him. And so Yusuf stays in that cell, forgotten by everyone, as the light crosses the wall, for several more years.
Seven fat cows, swallowed whole by seven starving ones. Seven green ears of grain, and seven dry. The king of Egypt wakes from the dream shaking, and no one in his court can explain it.

Golden fields, as far as the eye can see. The freed servant remembers Yusuf at last. Seven years of plenty are coming, he reads, then seven of famine. Plant all you can, and store it. It will keep your people alive.
The prison door swings open onto daylight. But Yusuf will not simply walk out. First, he says, ask about the women who cut their hands. Let my name be cleared. He will leave innocent, or not at all.

It is a patience almost beyond us. The Prophet, peace be upon him, said that had he waited in prison as long as Yusuf, and then been called, he would have hurried straight out. But Yusuf waited, for his honour.
The storehouses of all Egypt, mountains of grain, are placed into his hands. The boy who was thrown into a well, sold, and forgotten in a cell, now feeds an entire kingdom.
Then the earth cracks. The famine comes, just as he foretold, and spreads far beyond Egypt, until it reaches the hills of Canaan, a grieving old father, and ten desperate brothers.
Ten travel-worn men bow low before the most powerful man in Egypt, begging for grain, never once realizing they are looking into the eyes of the little brother they buried in a well.
He knows them at once. And to keep his youngest brother safely at his side, he hides the king's own cup inside the boy's sack. It was a plan, the Qur'an tells us, that Allah Himself taught him.

Back in Canaan, Yaqub still grieves. He has wept for Yusuf so long that his eyes have turned white. And still he will not lose hope. I only complain of my grief and my sorrow to Allah, he says. And I know from Allah what you do not know.

At last, broken and desperate, the brothers stand before him one final time. And he can hold it back no longer. I am Yusuf, he says. They freeze. And they know, in that instant, exactly what they have done.
They were completely in his power. He could have done anything to them. Instead, he said, there is no blame upon you today. May Allah forgive you. He is the most merciful of the merciful.

At the gates of Makkah, centuries later, a vast crowd waits. The Prophet, peace be upon him, stands over the very people who drove him out and killed his companions, and forgives them with almost the same words. Go, he says. You are free.

A white shirt lifts on the wind, carried home to Canaan. And before it even arrives, the old man lifts his head. I can sense the scent of Yusuf, he says, though you will think I am a foolish old man. They lay it over his face, and his sight comes rushing back.

Eleven stars, the sun, and the moon, rising over the horizon. His parents and his eleven brothers bow before him in honour. And the dream of that little boy comes true, exactly as he saw it, all those years before.
My Lord, You have given me power, and taught me. You are my protector in this world, and the next. Let me die in submission to You, and join me with the righteous.

A single trail of footprints, leading toward the sunrise. He was betrayed by his own blood, enslaved, lied about, and imprisoned for years. And every step was quietly carrying him exactly where Allah meant him to be. So whatever you are walking through tonight, do not lose hope. Trust the One who is writing your story.

sources: Qur'an: Surah Yusuf (chapter 12), text from Quran.com. Verses shown on screen with translation, never recited or synthesized: 12:4, 12:18, 12:39, 12:86, 12:92, 12:101 (plus 12:5, 12:15-18, 12:21, 12:23, 12:31-35, 12:41-50, 12:54-58, 12:70-76, 12:84, 12:87, 12:93-100 cited per beat in the foot line).

Hadith (authentic): Sahih al-Bukhari 3390 (Yusuf's lineage of four prophets: the noble son of the noble son of the noble son of the noble); Sahih al-Bukhari 3372 (the Prophet on Yusuf's patience: had he waited in prison as long as Yusuf, he would have answered the call at once); Sahih Muslim 162 (on the night journey, Yusuf had been given half of all beauty).

Seerah: at the Conquest of Makkah the Prophet, peace be upon him, forgave the Quraysh with 'Go, for you are free', echoing Yusuf's 'no blame upon you today' (12:92) - recorded in the books of seerah. No Israiliyyat or weak narrations are used; no embellishment beyond the Qur'anic and authentic hadith record. Visuals are real Pexels footage (human-free, text-free, frame-grab QC'd) except the code-generated Canaan->Egypt map.
